title: Energy harvesting system
abstract: Among other things, an integrated portable energy conversion mat is to be installed at a pavement surface over which a car or truck is to pass. The mat has a width at least as wide as the distance between the outermost edges of the treads of the tires located at an axle of the car or truck. The mat has a top surface over which the tires of the car or truck are to pass. The mat includes a sealed enclosed space housing and energy conversion system. The energy conversion system includes two sets of channels that extend along a length of the mat and contain hydraulic fluid under pressure. An energy converter converts energy carried by the hydraulic fluid in response to the tires of the car or truck passing over the top surface into electrical energy. An electrical port is coupled to the energy converter and exposed at an outer surface of the mat. The two sets of channels are spaced apart across the width of the mat by a distance that corresponds to the distance between the treads of the tires located at an axle of the car or truck. The energy converter is located within the sealed enclosed space of the mat and in the portion of the mat that is between the two sets of channels.
claims:
Claims What is claimed is: 1. An apparatus comprising a structure to receive kinetic energy from a wheel of a vehicle or a foot of a pedestrian, an energy converter, and a hydraulic system to transfer the received energy to the energy converter. 2. The apparatus of claim 1 in which the energy converter is configured to convert energy carried by hydraulic fluid of the hydraulic system into a different form of energy. 3. The apparatus of claim 2 in which the different form of energy comprises mechanical motion. 4. The apparatus of claim 3 in which the mechanical motion comprises rotational motion. 5. The apparatus of claim 3 in which the mechanical motion comprises linear motion. 6. The apparatus of claim 2 in which the energy converter is configured to convert the different form of energy into another form of energy. 7. The apparatus of claim 6 in which the other form of energy comprises electricity. 8. The apparatus of claim 1 in which the energy converter comprises an electric generator. 9. The apparatus of claim 1 in which the energy converter comprises at least one of a turbine, an impeller, and axial generator, a gerotor, and an expandable bellows. 10. The apparatus of claim 1 in which the energy converter comprises electricity distribution components. 11. The apparatus of claim 1 comprising a control system. 12. The apparatus of claim 11 in which the control system comprises sensors associated with a surface on which the wheel of the vehicle or the foot of the pedestrian is traveling. 13. The apparatus of claim 12 in which the sensors sense at least one of weight and speed of the vehicle or pedestrian. 14. The apparatus of claim 12 in which the control system comprises electronics that receive signals from the sensors and issue signals to control operation of at least one of the energy converter and the hydraulic system. 15. The apparatus of claim 13 in which the electronics improve energy efficiency of the operation of the apparatus based on the signals from the sensors. 16. The apparatus of claim 1 comprising wireless communication facilities to communicate information associated with the operation of the energy converter or the hydraulic system or both. 17. The apparatus of claim 16, in which the wireless communication facilities are configured to communicate the information to or from at least one of sensors configured to sense data associated with operation of the apparatus, wireless data transmission devices, a remote server, a remote computing device, a real time data dashboard, and a computing device configured to analyze data associated with operation of the apparatus. 18. The apparatus of claim 1 comprising data management facilities to manage data associated with the operation of the apparatus. 19. The apparatus of claim 18 in which the data management facilities comprise at least one of an emergency operation facility or a shutdown facility. 20. The apparatus of claim 18 in which the data management facilities comprise a real-time display representative of the operation of the apparatus. 21. The apparatus of claim 1 comprising a machine learning system configured to infer information about the vehicle or pedestrian and about other vehicles or pedestrians from home kinetic energy is being received. 22. The apparatus of claim 1 comprising an interface to at least one of a remote server, a remote controller, and a remote performance monitor. 23. The apparatus of claim 22, in which the interface is configured to transmit data to or receive data from at least one of the remote server, the remote controller, and the remote performance monitor. 24. The apparatus of claim 1 in which the energy converter comprises a rotating element coupled to an electric generator. 25. The apparatus of claim 1 in which the energy converter comprises a linear motion element coupled to an electric generator. 26. The apparatus of claim 1 in which the energy converter comprises an inlet port to receive hydraulic fluid. 27. The apparatus of claim 1 in which the energy converter comprises a housing. 28. The apparatus of claim 1 in which the energy converter comprises a nozzle for hydraulic fluid of the hydraulic system. 29. The apparatus of claim 1 in which the energy converter comprises a gerotor. 30. The apparatus of claim 1 in which the energy converter comprises a dual-action linear hydraulic piston. 31. The apparatus of claim 1 in which the energy converter comprises two or more energy conversion units in parallel or in series relative to the hydraulic system. 32. The apparatus of claim 31 in which the energy conversion units operate respectively with different operating characteristics. 33. The apparatus of claim 32 comprising a sensor to determine a characteristic of the vehicle or the pedestrian and provide a signal indicative of which energy conversion unit or units to activate. 34. The apparatus of claim 1 in which the energy converter comprises an outlet port for hydraulic fluid of the hydraulic system. 35. The apparatus of claim 1 in which the energy converter comprises elements to adjust the energy conversion structure dynamically to effect changes in operating characteristics. 36. The apparatus of claim 35 in which the elements comprise weights that slide along blades of an impeller depending on the rotational velocity of the impeller. 37. The apparatus of claim 35 in which the elements are configured to increase hydraulic pressures dynamically based on sensor signals. 38. The apparatus of claim 1 in which the energy receiving structure comprises a surface on which the wheel of the vehicle or the foot of the pedestrian makes contact. 39. The apparatus of claim 1 configured to be mounted in or on a roadway. 40. The apparatus of claim 1 in which the energy transferred by the hydraulic system is in the range of 50 Watts to 5000 Watts under continuous operation. 41. The apparatus of claim 1 in which the hydraulic system comprises a channel containing hydraulic fluid and having one end configured to enable compressed hydraulic fluid to apply a force to cause motion of the energy converter. 42. The apparatus of claim 1 in which the hydraulic system comprises a channel containing hydraulic fluid and having one end configured to apply force to the hydraulic fluid in response to the kinetic energy received from the wheel of the vehicle. 43. The apparatus of claim 1 in which the hydraulic system comprises a channel containing hydraulic fluid and configured to apply a continuing force to pump the hydraulic fluid in a preferential direction in the channel as a wheel of the vehicle moves along the structure that receives kinetic energy from the wheel. 44. The apparatus of claim 1 in which the hydraulic system comprises hydraulic channels containing pressurized hydraulic fluid and arranged so that the wheel of the vehicle or the foot of the pedestrian causes the hydraulic fluid to be pumped from an upstream end of the channels to a downstream end of the channels in a direction in which the vehicle or pedestrian is traveling. 45. The apparatus of claim 44 comprising a manifold coupling the channels at the downstream end. 46. The apparatus of claim 44 comprising a port having a one-way valve for adding hydraulic fluid to the channels. 47. The apparatus of claim 44 comprising a drain port having a one-way valve for draining fluid from the channels. 48. The apparatus of claim 1 in which the hydraulic system comprises a hydraulic shuttle path along which hydraulic fluid flows back and forth between two endpoints. 49. The apparatus of claim 48 in which the two endpoints are arranged so that one endpoint is nearer to an end of the apparatus that first receives the kinetic energy from the wheel of the vehicle or the foot of the pedestrian. 50. The apparatus of claim 48 in which the kinetic energy is transferred to the energy converter at a location that is between the two endpoints along the shuttle path. 51. The apparatus of claim 48 comprising a bypass return path parallel to the shuttle path for the hydraulic fluid to return to the first endpoint. 52. The apparatus of claim 48 in which the second endpoint comprises a device configured to receive the hydraulic fluid. 53. The apparatus of claim 52 in which the device configured to receive the hydraulic fluid comprises an accumulator. 54. The apparatus of claim 48 in which the first endpoint comprises ends of compressible channels. 55. The apparatus of claim 44 in which each of the hydraulic channels extends in the direction in which the vehicle or pedestrian is traveling, and the channels are arranged side-by-side across a width of the apparatus in each of at least one set of channels. 56. The apparatus of claim 55 in which adjacent channels are separated by vertical walls. 57. The apparatus of claim 56 in which each of the vertical walls has a thickness between 1/32 inch and Â¼ inch. 58. The apparatus of claim 57 in which the vertical walls are not stretchable. 59. The apparatus of claim 58 in which the vertical walls are non-elastic, flexible, and thin. 60. The apparatus of claim 57 in which the vertical walls are configured to bend when a force is applied to an upper surface of the apparatus. 61. The apparatus of claim 57 in which the vertical walls are have accordion bends. 62. The apparatus of claim 57 in which the vertical walls have a tensile strength between 16 megapascals and 100 megapascals. 63. The apparatus of claim 57 comprising transverse features that are bendable in response to the force of the vehicle or the pedestrian. 64. The apparatus of claim 55 in which an aggregate width spanned by the hydraulic channels of one of the sets, across the width of the apparatus, is at least as wide as a tread of the wheel or the width of the shoe. 65. The apparatus of claim 44 in which the number of channels across the width of the apparatus is at least 8 per foot. 66. The apparatus of claim 65 in which the width of each of the channels across the width of the apparatus is less than 3 inches. 67. The apparatus of claim 65 in which the width of each of the channels across the width of the apparatus is less than 1 inch. 68. The apparatus of claim 65 in which each of the channels can withstand a pressure of up to 200 pounds per square inch of the hydraulic fluid in the channel. 69. The apparatus of claim 1 in which hydraulic system at rest is pressurized at a pressure in the range of 2 pounds per square inch to 200 pounds per square inch. 70. The apparatus of claim 44 in which there are two sets of channels separated by a section along a length of the apparatus that does not contain channels. 71. The apparatus of claim 70 in which at least part of the energy converter or part of the hydraulic accumulator or both are located in the section. 72. The apparatus of claim 44 comprising a top that forms upper walls of the channels. 73. The apparatus of claim 44 comprising a bottom that forms bottom walls of the channels. 74. The apparatus of claim 44 in which the vertical walls begin at first ends of the channels and seal the first ends of the channels from one another and end at a location spaced apart from second ends of the channels to enable hydraulic fluid to flow between the channels at the second ends. 75. The apparatus of claim 74 in which the second ends of the channels form an integrated port for the hydraulic fluid to flow to the energy converter. 76. The apparatus of claim 44 in which the cross-section of each of the channels varies along the length of the channel to tend to maximize flow of hydraulic fluid toward the energy converter and to maintain low or steady hydraulic pressures. 77. The apparatus of claim 44 in which the channels are configured to undergo complete collapse of the chamber under the force of the vehicle or the pedestrian. 78. The apparatus of claim 44 in which each of the channels is partitioned vertically into a stack of sub-channels. 79. The apparatus of claim 44 in which each of the channels comprises transverse features configured to tend to maximize the flow of hydraulic fluid toward the energy converter. 80. The apparatus of claim 79 in which the transverse features comprise a series of collapsible gates that cooperate to collapse in sequence as the vehicle or pedestrian moves across the apparatus. 81. The apparatus of claim 80 in which the gates comprise elements coupled to side or top walls or both of the channels. 82. The apparatus of claim 80 in which the gates comprise flaps attached to bottom walls of the channels. 83. The apparatus of claim 1 comprising interlocking zipper features along a length of the apparatus. 84. The apparatus of claim 83 in which the interlocking zipper features are located inside channels of the hydraulic system. 85. The apparatus of claim 1 comprising channels having undulating channel walls. 86. The apparatus of claim 1 in which the hydraulic system comprises a nozzle to cause the hydraulic fluid to apply the force. 87. The apparatus of claim 1 in which the hydraulic system comprises a sealed recirculation system containing hydraulic fluid and configured to apply force to the hydraulic fluid in response to kinetic energy received from the wheel of the vehicle, to enable the hydraulic fluid to apply a corresponding force to cause motion of the energy converter, and to recirculate hydraulic fluid that has applied the force to cause the motion of the energy converter so that the sealed recirculation system can again apply force to the hydraulic fluid in response to kinetic energy received from the wheel of the vehicle or a wheel of another vehicle. 88. The apparatus of claim 1 in which the hydraulic system is pressurized. 89. The apparatus of claim 1 comprising a series of interconnected rigid plates on the structure that receives kinetic energy. 90. The apparatus of claim 1 comprising a chamber to receive hydraulic fluid of the hydraulic system after it has transferred the received energy to the energy converter. 91. The apparatus of claim 90 in which the chamber comprises a bladder accumulator. 92. The apparatus of claim 91 in which the bladder accumulator comprises a bladder, a rigid enclosure for the bladder, and a compressible filler between the bladder and the rigid enclosure or inside the bladder. 93. The apparatus of claim 91 in which the compressible filler is configured to be matched to the vehicle or the pedestrian. 94. The apparatus of claim 1 in which the hydraulic system comprises a path sealed at both ends and the energy converter is either (i) at a location along the path between the ends or (ii) at one of the ends of the path. 95. The apparatus of claim 94 in which the path is linear. 96. The apparatus of claim 94 in which the path is non-linear. 97. The apparatus of claim 94 in which one end of the path comprises an accumulator. 98. The apparatus of claim 94 in which a portion of the path before the location of the energy converter extends along a direction traveled by the vehicle or the pedestrian and a portion of the path after the location of the energy converter extends in a direction opposite the traveled direction. 99. The apparatus of claim 94 in which an accumulator is located at one end or the other end or both ends of the path. 100. The apparatus of claim 1 comprising two or more modular units. 101. The apparatus of claim 100 in which the two or more modular units are mechanically coupled to one another. 102. The apparatus of claim 100 in which the two or more modular units are connected electrically to one another and together are connected by a single electrical connection to a load. 103. The apparatus of claim 100 in which the two or more modular units are not connected hydraulically to one another. 104. The apparatus of claim 100 in which the two or more modular units are electrically coupled directly or indirectly to one another. 105. The apparatus of claim 1 in which the apparatus comprises a unit that is modular with respect to one or more other modular units with which the apparatus can operate cooperatively. 106. The apparatus of claim 1 comprising an integrated sealed unit. 107. The apparatus of claim 1 comprising a portable unit. 108. The apparatus of claim 1 comprising a mat. 109. The apparatus of claim 108 in which the mat comprises a durable resilient material that can be compressed between 1 million and 100 million times before failing. 110. The apparatus of claim 108 in which the mat is rectangular. 111. The apparatus of claim 108 in which the mat comprises an external surface exposed to the environment and internal chambers to house the energy converter and the hydraulic system. 112. The apparatus of claim 1 having a length between 1 foot and 6 feet and a width between 1 foot and 15 feet, and a thickness between Â¼ inch and 5 inches. 113. The apparatus of claim 112 in which the thickness of the apparatus is between Â½ inch and 2 Â½ inches. 114. The apparatus of claim 108 in which the mat comprises an upper surface to receive the kinetic energy from the wheel of the vehicle or the foot of the pedestrian, and the upper surface has a contour that is other than flat. 115. The apparatus of claim 108 in which the mat is configured to lie on a travel surface along which a vehicle having a wheel or a pedestrian can travel. 116. The apparatus of claim 108 in which the mat is configured to be embedded within pavement bearing a travel surface along which a vehicle having a wheel or a pedestrian can travel. 117. The apparatus of claim 1 in which the structure to receive the kinetic energy and the energy converter are housed in separate housings and are connected by a channel of the hydraulic system. 118. The apparatus of claim 1 in which the structure to receive the kinetic energy is configured to be compressed by the wheel of the vehicle or the foot of the pedestrian and thereafter to rebound before a wheel of another vehicle or another foot of a pedestrian causes the structure to be compressed. 119. An apparatus comprising a set of modular energy conversion units each having a structure to receive kinetic energy from a wheel of a vehicle or a foot of a pedestrian, and 

 an energy harvesting system that includes a hydraulic system, to convert the kinetic energy to electricity to be delivered at an electrical interface, the electrical interfaces of the units being coupled to provide electricity to a load. 120. An apparatus comprising a travel surface along which a vehicle having a wheel or a pedestrian can travel, the travel surface including an energy receiving surface over which the wheel or a foot of the pedestrian passes as the vehicle or pedestrian travels along the travel surface, the energy receiving surface being part of an energy harvesting system that receives kinetic energy from the wheel and hydraulically transfers it to an energy converter for conversion to motion of the energy converter. 121. The apparatus of claim 120 in which the travel surface is on pavement and the energy harvesting system is embedded in the pavement with the energy receiving surface exposed at the travel surface. 122. The apparatus of claim 120 in which the energy receiving surface is flush with the travel surface. 123. The apparatus of claim 120 in which the energy receiving surface is raised above the travel surface. 124. The apparatus of claim 120 in which the energy harvesting system comprises a mat bearing the energy receiving surface and embedded in the pavement or disposed on the pavement, another part of the hydraulic system and the energy converter being separately embedded in the pavement or disposed on the pavement. 125. The apparatus of claim 120 in which the travel surface is on pavement and the energy harvesting system comprises a portable unit temporarily positioned on the travel surface. 126. The apparatus of claim 1 or 120 in which the kinetic energy receiving structure is at a location at which the vehicle decelerates or stops. 127. The apparatus of claim 1 or 120 in which the kinetic energy receiving structure is at a location that is temporary. 128. An apparatus comprising a rectangular energy harvesting unit configured to lie on or be embedded within pavement that bears a travel surface for a pedestrian or a vehicle that has wheels, the energy harvesting unit having an upper surface that is exposed at the travel surface, the upper surface bearing readable markings that are visible from the vehicle or to the pedestrian, the upper surface being sufficiently durable to withstand the passage over the upper surface of 1 million to 100 million vehicles or pedestrians, the upper surface having a predetermined frictional coefficient relative to the wheels or to feet of the pedestrian, and structural elements to protect the apparatus from damage by snowplows. 129. An apparatus comprising an integrated portable energy conversion mat for installation at a pavement surface over which a vehicle is to pass, the mat having a width at least as wide as the distance between the outermost edges of the treads of the tires located at an axle of the vehicle, the mat having a top surface over which the tires of the vehicle are to pass, the mat comprising a sealed enclosed space housing and energy conversion system, the energy conversion system comprising two sets of channels that extend along a length of the mat and contain hydraulic fluid under pressure, an energy converter to convert energy carried by the hydraulic fluid in response to the tires of the vehicle passing over the top surface into electrical energy, an electrical port coupled to the energy converter and exposed at an outer surface of the mat, 

 the two sets of channels being spaced apart across the width of the mat by a distance that corresponds to the distance between the treads of the tires located at an axle of the vehicle, and the energy converter being located within the sealed enclosed space of the mat and in the portion of the mat that is between the two sets of channels.
